speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about how the, how the [INAUDIBLE] or-- you're not quite sure who had pulled all the Jews out from your street. And they were cleaning under the cars.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes,

speaker: interviewer
text: And so your parents told you--

speaker: interviewee
text: That one of our neighbors, an elderly gentleman who owned a toy store two houses down the street, he was pulled out also and forced under the truck to clean up. And his son, who was also gotten in the same store, was given a hose from a fire hydrant and told to help his father and the other people on the knees to clean the car. And he refused to douse his father. His father was-- had a weak heart or something. And the youngster refused to do. So they beat the youngster up and took him over here. I don't know what happened to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you did not see this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not. That was reported to me by my parents. That happened during the day while I was away from home. Who that people-- who that person was who warned us, we don't know. We never found out. Was a lucky coincidence.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about any markings on any papers or anything that you had to wear on your, on your clothing? Is there anything that--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I, I was not there any more over in the yellow star became mandatory. That happened shortly after the Kristallnacht. But I was gone by then. But of course, I was desperately trying to go somewhere. My brother, through some connection, had gotten to the United States at the end of April. He and his wife with all his belongings was able to get to the United States at the end of April.

speaker: interviewer
text: So literally six weeks after the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anschluss.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. At that time, the restrictions were not enforced yet completely. That got worse and worse every week. But we, of course-- I couldn't go to a movie anymore. I couldn't use my bicycle. I could not use my kayak. The Danube, the Vienna boats were forbidden to Jews. Most movies, most restaurants, most coffee houses had signs-- lepers, dogs, and Jews not allowed, in that order. [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were strictly confined to our immediate families or friends. Because we didn't dare getting more than, let's say, six people in one place at one time. Somebody might have seen people going up there, denounced us, and trouble. So we were depending on ourselves. And, of course, we tried everything to find where could we go? And to my brother in the United States helped. He got me a permit and a visa to Cuba, to Havana. And the only trouble was that they had confiscated my passport when they were visiting us on that Monday. And it was difficult to get another passport.

speaker: interviewee
text: The lines in front of the American consulate wound around the block. And Nazi hoodlums with or without uniform, with or without swastika armbands, were at will bothering those people. Because they knew they're all Jews. And finally, another connection-- a Jewish lawyer who had somehow gotten a connection. He was a classmate of mine who had gotten into law. He had some connection. He was able for some extra good money for various people getting passports a little faster. Normally, you had to stand to get a number. Then a few days later, you went with the number, stood in line again. And you got some forms to fill in. A few days later, you handed in the forms. And every time you had to spend a day standing in line and being bothered. He was able, with some money, to cut that short. And I got the passport.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your parents and your girlfriend, Adele?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- Adele had a-- had found, in the correspondence left by her parents, a letter with an address of somebody in Brooklyn. Turned out it was a cousin of hers who had-- before 1920-- had written a letter. And she had the address. She wrote to him. And he sent her an affidavit. And she was early enough. And she was an early quota number. And her quota was due in beginning of October, 1938. My brother, by the time he got himself to America and found somebody to give me an affidavit-- because he couldn't. He had nothing to back him up. By the time I got to the point, my quota number was due sometimes late in 1939. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: In the meantime in Vienna, in the newspaper, appeared an article, Jewish men who want to emigrate have to get clearance from the Austrian-- oh, excuse me-- German army by then. So up you went to the nearest armory. There sat a young German lieutenant, smirking at you. What can we do to you today? And I told him, there is the article. So you want permission to emigrate? Yes. So I got the document. I still have it-- or copies. And that was restricted. It expires on September 30, 1938. In my innocence, I asked the man, where can I get an extension? The answer was, quote, dumb Jew. If you are not out of the country by then, the local police, who gets a copy of this permit, will pick you up at home. And you can figure out where you are going-- Dachau or whatever. So there I was. Where do I go? Well, my brother helped. He bought-- for $500 at that time, which his boss lent him-- bought me a visa for Cuba. Now he had to scurry around to find transportation. And he found the Italian ships line, which had a berth available from Genoa to Cuba on December the 6th, 1938. I had to be out of Austria on September 30. What do I do from December 30 till December 6? I had very good friends in Denmark. I had lived in Denmark prior to that for a while after, after my graduation from school.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wrote to them. They tried their best. They couldn't do anything. But at least I found out that with a German passport-- the Austrian was gone-- I did not need a visa to Denmark. So I took a chance. I said, I'm going to Denmark. I'll see what I can do there. I spoke perfectly Danish, almost without an accent from the time I had lived there. So I got my things together-- two little suitcases-- and said goodbye to my family, got on a train to Berlin first. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you pack in the suitcases? Anything of note?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of my technical papers, my slide rules, some drafting instruments-- I hoped to be employed in my-- in my line-- and some spare underwear, a pair of extra shoes, an extra suit. And that that's pretty much all.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about family photographs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Almost none-- my mother was able to get all those things out later on. Anyway, on the trip to Berlin, I rode third class, which was standard. And I shared the compartment with, I think, six or seven young men slightly older than I but in that general range, most of them wearing swastikas on their lapel, which I couldn't do. And I tried not to talk too much. But I had put on this little [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] athletic sign, which I have here-- the, the small one. Yes, this one. And that was well-known. People knew when you had that, you were physically capable and recognized.

speaker: interviewer
text: You want to hold that up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most-- this--

speaker: interviewer
text: You have to hold it-- remember, we said hold it like this, close to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This worn one on your-- this was worn on your training suit, on your active athletic equipment. Also, most of the police in Vienna wore that as sign that they had passed the physical activity test. Now, the smaller version, which was given to us at the same time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wore on my lapel. And that diverted the discussion from politics to athletics. There, I could talk, didn't have to hesitate. And officially, I went to Denmark to be present at the wedding of my sort of-- well, during the war, we had what we call the-- the Danes had accepted many of the Austrian children to help out.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the kinder transports.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. Yeah. Children's transport-- and I said I had met a family there. And the daughter is getting married. I am going to be there. Fine. One of the comments was don't delay too much. The preparation for Czechoslovakia are progressing very fast, end of September, 1938. Anyway, I came to Berlin, spent a little time, finally got on the airport, and took my first airplane flight, a 24-seater prop engine to Denmark.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Berlin like, though? Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: How many-- were you just in Berlin for a few hours?

speaker: interviewee
text: For a few hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We landed about mid morning. And late afternoon, I had a bike in the, in the airport, walked around. I didn't want to go anywhere. Because it was, of course, full of, full of uniform and Nazis. It was Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about banners and things like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Banners, flags all over, and also in Vienna-- all the houses had to have a Nazi flag. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And should I backtrack again one event? All right. We are still in Berlin but backtracking to one Sunday, the second Sunday in Vienna after the Anschluss. A Nazi in civilian dress came to our apartment. We know that you have a sewing shop down there. We want you to go down and sew a Nazi flag, because your apartment house doesn't have one. And of course, you will supply the material. They knew that we had that type.

speaker: interviewer
text: So mother and I went down into our shop. We had to supply some big, red material. I had to cut the white circle with the black swastika. Mother had to sit at the machine and sew it together. Thank you. You could go back to our apartment. And they hoisted the flag in front of our house-- was a, a four-story apartment house and, and across from the-- from our store. To come back to-- Berlin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Berlin, I got on the plane, was very comfortable and very interesting, my first flight. In Copenhagen, I disembarked and-- or dis-planed. And to my delight, quote unquote, two exits-- one Scandinavian citizen, the other, non-Scandinavian citizen or passports. Of course, I had to go there. They looked at my passport and said, well, we have to inform you that you cannot consider yourself as having landed in Denmark. You are in no, no-man's land. And we cannot guarantee you that we are going to accept you. The first thing they wanted me to sign was a waiver-- was to get me a translator, which I declined. I spoke perfectly Danish. And I told them so. I spoke to them in Danish. And they made me sign a waiver that they were free.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from then on, we spoke Danish. And they were very helpful and very nice, wanted to know how I had learned my Danish and so on. I told them my story from 1931, when I had lived up there. Even at that time, '31, had a little run-in with the local police. I had worked, got caught. And an entry in my passport then was work permission denied, signed, police in Denmark.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I mentioned the fact to the police at the airport. Anyway, they detained me, transported me to a police detention facility in Copenhagen. There, they took our belts, our neckties, our shoelaces away, had us finger printed, and then sent us upstairs into one of the dormitories. I say us, because on the plane-- same plane-- unknown to me was another young man about my age who also was denied landing. Now, I had told them my entire story-- that I had a landing permit in Cuba, that I had round-trip tickets to Berlin, Copenhagen, to Antwerp, to Genoa, and to Havana. You had to have round-trip tickets, because under the law, if your destiny station does not allow you to get there, the transportation company has to take you back where you came from.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all the transportation lines make big business charging you twice and give us no refund, of course. So I had a return trip ticket from Cuba to Vienna. But anyway, the one helped me. And the interviewing police officer told me that they are going to check my information. They are going to check my documents and also the information about the police problem in 1931.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the meantime, if my story doesn't hold up on the first plane at 6:00 in the morning, we are going to be flown back to Berlin. They don't-- there's nothing they can do. They are worried. They would like to help us. But they are worried what the big neighbor to the south will do to them. They got the experience a little bit later. And he came up once late at night, around 10:00 or so, and said they have checked out my information. Everything is apparently in order. In the morning, they will decide what will happen to me. In the meantime, he said, don't get scared. We are going to pick up that other young man. He's going back, because he gave us a story which doesn't hold water. And we can't keep him. I wanted to talk to the man who brought us our dinner. He told me he's not allowed to talk to prisoners, in Danish. And that was that. So he bought me my-- and around 4:00 in the morning, they came, picked up that other boy, told me to go back to sleep, not to make any trouble. And from then until about 6 o'clock at night, I was in that dormitory with 24 beds-- empty-- wash basins along a one wall, no newspaper, no books, nothing. For my own, my luggage was kept by the police. I had absolutely nothing. So I marched back and forth and back and forth. Harder time went by. It was very difficult. By around 6 o'clock, the same officer who had interviewed me at the airport came in beaming, congratulating me. My story had-- was approved and there was given permission to stay until a ship would take me to Antwerp from Esbjerg on the west coast of Denmark. In the meantime, I was free in Copenhagen to do whatever I had to do. But they had gotten me something I was not able to get in Vienna-- a Belgian and French transit visa, which I needed and which I had hoped to get in Denmark on my own. They had gotten it for me in order to get rid of me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they gave me exact instructions first to go to the Belgian embassy to get the transit visa, then go to the French embassy where, on the basis of the Belgian transit visa, they would give me the French transit visa. The ticket for Italy, I had. So I was OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came. I came to-- I came to, to Esbjerg. There was a policeman waiting at the pier, checked his paper against my passport, stayed with me on board ship until they left-- made sure that I didn't get off. And I sailed to Antwerp. On the board-- on board ship, I found out-- no, that was still in, still in Esbjerg-- I found out that there would be a train waiting at the landing in Antwerp, which would take me directly to the French border. I would not be allowed to stay in Belgium. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I had a made up form-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I got a little bit confused now. Somehow I had notified my family of the trips. And as soon as Adele found out in Vienna that I would be in Antwerp, she, who, who left for the United States from Antwerp, went to Antwerp and had rented a hotel room. And when my boat was supposed to land-- you know, that happened while I had four days in Denmark that I sent those telegrams. And she had gotten down to the, to the, to the port and started a little-- this little play with some of the customs officers-- a few tears and little lamentation that her fiance is not allowed. And she talked him into that I could slip through. I didn't have to go directly to the train to the border. He let me slip into Antwerp. Antwerp at that time was overrun by Eastern European or by-- you know, it was, from Antwerp, it's Eastern European [INAUDIBLE]. The first morning, of course, when she got me off the port into Antwerp, we went to hotel, the first time that I could breathe. That's the beginning of my freedom. And the next morning, we went down to the police and got official residency permit-- temporary, of course. No problem for her-- she had her ticket for a few days later with the trip ticket. For me, it was a little bit difficult. I had a temporary residence permit in Antwerp. But again, my French helped me tremendously. I didn't have to speak German to those people. Belgium had no use for the Germans. They remembered the First World War. They knew what was happening. They expected the invasion. And so speaking French to them was a tremendous help. I spent two months in Antwerp. And in the meantime, Adele had sailed and started in Brooklyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you then make it to Genoa fairly easily from Antwerp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I made, I made it from Antwerp first to Paris. In Paris, I was picked up by a young French grade school teacher who I had helped a few years before. Well, he had gotten lost and lost his passport in Vienna. Again, French-speaking-- he didn't speak German. He only spoke French. He happened to approach me in the street. He looked helpless. I helped him. I took him home, fed him. He slept in our house for a few days. We chipped in the money, bought him the tickets back to Paris. And we got a wonderful letter from his mother with promise of whenever we needed help.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from Antwerp, I had written to him. He was at the station in Paris, took me home to his parents. They were both working people. But at least I had a steady home. He had-- he couldn't take off from teaching because he had lost a lot of time during the [INAUDIBLE] mobilization of the French army the year before. You know about that? Right. But I think we need to keep directly on your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I spent a week in Paris, then went on the train to, to Genoa and stayed in Genoa, got to the-- got to a hotel and found out that my ship hadn't come yet. So I I went to the ship's line. That's when I found out the ship hadn't come to Genoa and would be at least 10 days before I could board the ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I asked the man there. I had been cleared in Antwerp. I made sure in Antwerp at the ship's line that my boat-- that my ticket was all right. And he had looked up and said, it's all right. You are booked for December 6.

speaker: interviewee
text: And consequently, I had left Antwerp and all that, figuring it's December 6th, I'll go on the boat in Genoa. The boat hadn't come yet from its trip to Mexico. So the ship's line, they had no choice. They got me into the local hotel, paid my room and board, and took my address. And finally, a few days later, when the ship finally came in, I got notified. My ship is here. I should be, at a given time, there at the pier for embarkation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, We have to change the reel.

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to change the reel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.
